2026-04-24 1:45 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

2026-04-24 1:45 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 1:45 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 1:46 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 1:46 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 1:46 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`

The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)

3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 1:47 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 1:47 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 1:47 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 1:56 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

**Your permission toggle frustration is the same root cause.** Each rebuild without a stable identity gets a fresh ad-hoc cdhash, and macOS drops the TCC grants tied to the old one. You grant, rebuild, toggle vanishes. That is the hundred-times loop.

So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)
2. Paste into MBP Terminal, run
3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 1:56 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 1:57 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 1:58 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. **The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

2026-04-24 1:58 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 1:58 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)

3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 2:00 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 2:00 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 2:00 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:03 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**Running process IS on the fresh binary.** FreeFlow Dev started at 11:52 AM today, binary built at 08:25 AM — so the running process has the `writeRecordingStateFlag` code baked in. The rebuild won't magically add missing logic; the logic is already there.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

1. Show you the 10 local commits and the 5 upstream commits you are missing — one minute look, catch any gotcha

2026-04-24 2:03 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

2026-04-24 2:04 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:07 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:07 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 2:07 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

2026-04-24 2:09 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 2:09 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:10 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:10 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 2:10 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 2:10 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:11 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:11 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:12 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

The rebuild would not have fixed this — it is a permissions issue, not a code issue.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

2026-04-24 2:13 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**Running process IS on the fresh binary.** FreeFlow Dev started at 11:52 AM today, binary built at 08:25 AM — so the running process has the `writeRecordingStateFlag` code baked in. The rebuild won't magically add missing logic; the logic is already there.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

1. Show you the 10 local commits and the 5 upstream commits you are missing — one minute look, catch any gotcha
2. `make clean && make` in `~/apps/freeflow/`
3. `kill 848` (FreeFlow Dev) and relaunch

**That is the answer.** FreeFlow Dev is missing Screen Recording and Accessibility permissions. Without Accessibility it cannot intercept the Option hotkey globally or paste transcribed text, which means `isRecording` never flips true for a full dictation cycle, which means the flag file never gets written, which means barge-in is dead. The rebuild would not have fixed this — it is a permissions issue, not a code issue.

**Build failed:** `FreeFlow Dev: no identity found` — the self-signed codesign certificate named "FreeFlow Dev" is missing from your keychain. That is what lets every build share the same cryptographic identity so TCC permissions persist.

**Your permission toggle frustration is the same root cause.** Each rebuild without a stable identity gets a fresh ad-hoc cdhash, and macOS drops the TCC grants tied to the old one. You grant, rebuild, toggle vanishes. That is the hundred-times loop.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

2026-04-24 2:13 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 2:13 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

2026-04-24 2:33 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 2:33 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 2:33 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:55 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:55 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 2:56 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 2:58 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. **The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

2026-04-24 2:58 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

2026-04-24 3:00 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**Running process IS on the fresh binary.** FreeFlow Dev started at 11:52 AM today, binary built at 08:25 AM — so the running process has the `writeRecordingStateFlag` code baked in. The rebuild won't magically add missing logic; the logic is already there.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

1. Show you the 10 local commits and the 5 upstream commits you are missing — one minute look, catch any gotcha
2. `make clean && make` in `~/apps/freeflow/`
3. `kill 848` (FreeFlow Dev) and relaunch
4. Live test: I tail the flag path while you dictate — see if the file blinks
5. If flag appears but barge-in still fails, the bug is in speech-server; if flag never appears even on a fresh build, bug is in FreeFlow

2026-04-24 3:00 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 12:46 PM]

**That is the answer.** FreeFlow Dev is missing Screen Recording and Accessibility permissions. Without Accessibility it cannot intercept the Option hotkey globally or paste transcribed text, which means `isRecording` never flips true for a full dictation cycle, which means the flag file never gets written, which means barge-in is dead. The rebuild would not have fixed this — it is a permissions issue, not a code issue.

## Fix

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)

4. Build finishes. Tell me.

7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 3:00 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 3:00 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

2026-04-24 3:01 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 3:01 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 3:01 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 3:02 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)
2. Paste into MBP Terminal, run
3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 3:02 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 3:03 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 3:04 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

1. Show you the 10 local commits and the 5 upstream commits you are missing — one minute look, catch any gotcha
2. `make clean && make` in `~/apps/freeflow/`

**Your permission toggle frustration is the same root cause.** Each rebuild without a stable identity gets a fresh ad-hoc cdhash, and macOS drops the TCC grants tied to the old one. You grant, rebuild, toggle vanishes. That is the hundred-times loop.

2026-04-24 3:04 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 3:04 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 3:04 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 3:05 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 3:05 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 3:06 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.
