2026-04-24 5:14 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 5:16 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 5:16 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude: Found a couple of things before we rebuild.

**Running process IS on the fresh binary.** FreeFlow Dev started at 11:52 AM today, binary built at 08:25 AM — so the running process has the `writeRecordingStateFlag` code baked in. The rebuild won't magically add missing logic; the logic is already there.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

1. Show you the 10 local commits and the 5 upstream commits you are missing — one minute look, catch any gotcha
2. `make clean && make` in `~/apps/freeflow/`
3. `kill 848` (FreeFlow Dev) and relaunch
4. Live test: I tail the flag path while you dictate — see if the file blinks
5. If flag appears but barge-in still fails, the bug is in speech-server; if flag never appears even on a fresh build, bug is in FreeFlow

Want me to show you the divergence first, or just blast through clean/rebuild/relaunch and skip straight to the test?

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 12:46 PM]

**That is the answer.** FreeFlow Dev is missing Screen Recording and Accessibility permissions. Without Accessibility it cannot intercept the Option hotkey globally or paste transcribed text, which means `isRecording` never flips true for a full dictation cycle, which means the flag file never gets written, which means barge-in is dead. The rebuild would not have fixed this — it is a permissions issue, not a code issue.

## Fix

Opening both System Settings panes on MBP so you can tick the boxes.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)

3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 5:19 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

2026-04-24 5:20 PM  Claude: Seeing the conundrum — Wispr Flow works but is not ours, FreeFlow is ours but half-finished. You want the barge-in signal to be rock solid regardless of which you use. Here is where that sits:

## What I found in the code

- FreeFlow Dev IS running (PID 848), built fresh today at 08:25 AM
- The code is wired correctly — `isRecording`'s didSet calls `writeRecordingStateFlag`, which writes a timestamp to `~/Library/Application Support/FreeFlow Dev/is-recording`
- speech-server.py watches that exact path (plus the non-dev one) and triggers `barge_in()` when it appears
- **BUT** the flag file does not exist right now, and `mic-active` binary returns `idle`

That by itself is not proof of brokenness — if you are not dictating, the flag should be absent. The real test is a live dictation: if FreeFlow writes the flag, it briefly appears; if the flag never appears while you dictate, FreeFlow is not triggering the write.

## The second half of the conundrum

Even when FreeFlow writes the flag, there is a deliberate **echo-gate** in speech-server.py ([speech-server.py:413](apps/voice-pipeline/bin/speech-server.py#L413)) that suppresses mic-based barge-in while audio is playing — to stop the TTS output from barging itself via mic bleed. The FreeFlow flag is supposed to bypass the echo-gate (explicit user intent), but mic-active alone will not. So if FreeFlow isn't firing, you are left with a signal that gets ignored during speech — exactly the symptom you described.

## The debug I want to run

I will watch the flag file path on MBP for 10 seconds. You start dictating with FreeFlow during that window. If the file appears → FreeFlow is working, the problem is elsewhere (server logic, kill latency, audio queue). If it never appears → FreeFlow is the culprit and we fix the Swift code.

Ready when you are. Tell me to start the watch and then dictate.

**Running process IS on the fresh binary.** FreeFlow Dev started at 11:52 AM today, binary built at 08:25 AM — so the running process has the `writeRecordingStateFlag` code baked in. The rebuild won't magically add missing logic; the logic is already there.

**But freeflow repo is diverged: 10 ahead, 5 behind upstream.** That is worth understanding before rebuilding. If your 10 local commits include a fix and your 5 missing upstream commits include a regression, you want to know which.

## Rebuild plan

Most likely cause: **Screen Recording permission is still missing**, and "Global shortcuts unavailable" is still showing in the FreeFlow menu. Without those, the hotkey never reaches FreeFlow, `isRecording` stays false, and no flag ever gets written.

Can you check:
1. **Screen Recording** granted for FreeFlow Dev? (different pane from Accessibility — I opened it earlier)
2. Does the FreeFlow menu bar still show "Screen Recording Permission Needed" or "Global shortcuts unavailable"? Or have those warnings cleared?

Once both permissions are green and the warnings are gone, redo the dictation test. If FreeFlow still does not write the flag with full permissions, then we dig into the Swift code.

Good flag — Xcode context matters. Short answer though: **we are already using Xcode's signing identity.** "Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" is the cert Xcode installs when you log into your developer account. So having Xcode installed is how we got here; it is not an unused tool.

Where Xcode *would* change the picture is if FreeFlow had an `.xcodeproj` or `Package.swift`. It has neither — just a Makefile that calls `swiftc` directly. To use Xcode's "click Run, everything works" flow you would first have to convert the project to SPM or make an xcodeproj wrapper. That is 30+ minutes of conversion work and not the bottleneck.

**The real bottleneck was the keychain prompt from SSH.** That is why the build failed for me earlier but succeeded when you ran it locally. Once you click **Always Allow** on that prompt (ONE time), future SSH rebuilds work and we do not hit this again.

## Fastest path from where we are right now

You are 2 minutes from a clean working FreeFlow Dev:

1. The clipboard on MBP has the rebuild command (`cd ~/apps/freeflow && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development..." make`)
2. Paste into MBP Terminal, run
3. Keychain prompt appears → click **Always Allow** (not just Allow)
4. Build finishes. Tell me.
5. I launch. Fresh permission prompts appear (Screen Recording, Accessibility, etc.)
6. Click Allow on each as they pop
7. Dictate. Barge-in either works or I dig into the Swift code

If this fails, we pivot to making an Xcode project. But I would not do the conversion preemptively — let us see if the clean reinstall works first.

2026-04-24 5:20 PM  Claude: Two things to address:

1. **Two different FreeFlow Devs on your machine.** The newer one (today 1:02 PM) is the rebuilt binary we want. The Apr 21 one is a stale older copy somewhere else. System Settings is showing you both as options and they have different codesign hashes — macOS treats them as different apps for TCC. If you enable permissions for the wrong one, it does nothing for the running one.

2. **Toggles not sticking** is a classic macOS TCC symptom when two binaries with the same bundle ID and different cdhashes conflict. Resetting TCC alone does not fix this while both copies exist.

Let me find the stale Apr 21 copy and nuke it:

2026-04-24 5:20 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Find all FreeFlow Dev.app copies

2026-04-24 5:20 PM  Claude: Answering each:

**1. Why two FreeFlow Devs.** The old Apr 21 one is in `/Users/ojhurst/apps/freeflow.scrapped-20260423-121750/build/FreeFlow Dev.app` — a scrapped directory from April 23. Same bundle ID, different cdhash. That is exactly why System Settings is flaky on you.

**2. Why `/Applications` sometimes works automatically.** When you build into `~/apps/freeflow/build/`, macOS does not auto-list it in System Settings. The `+` button requires you to hunt for it. If the app lives in `/Applications/`, Launch Services knows about it immediately and System Settings auto-surfaces it. Easy fix: copy the built app to `/Applications` after each build.

**3. Why toggles do not stick.** Classic TCC bug when two binaries share a bundle ID. macOS keeps looking at the wrong cdhash. Until the scrapped copy is gone, this will keep happening.

**4. Wipe everything and start over — YES.** That is the clean move.

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:33 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:40 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:05 PM]
Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:40 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:40 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

2026-04-24 5:43 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:43 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:44 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:44 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:47 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:47 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

2026-04-24 5:48 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:05 PM]
Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:48 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:48 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:49 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:49 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

2026-04-24 5:55 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:05 PM]
Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:55 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:55 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

**What to look for:**
- Quick dictation (short phrase) — waveform freezes briefly, text inserts, no spinner. Stays as it was before.
- Longer dictation (or a slow model response) — after about one second of processing, the waveform swaps to a small white circular spinner. That is the new state.

Try it and tell me if the timing feels right or if one second is too long or too short. If you prefer to test on the MBP instead, say the word and I will push this branch up so you can pull there.

2026-04-24 5:56 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:56 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:57 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:57 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 5:59 PM  James: already checked for some reason

2026-04-24 5:59 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 5:59 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 6:03 PM  Claude: Two things you spotted, both real:

**1. Setup wizard exists.** I missed that — apologies. The fix is much smaller than I said: just auto-launch the existing setup wizard on cold start when permissions or API key are missing. That is a 5-line change in App.swift, not a UX rebuild. File as a separate PR after we are done with the spinner.

**2. Toggle does nothing.** Classic stale TCC entry. The row is left over from a prior build with a different signature, so flipping the toggle is operating on a ghost. The fix:

- In that Accessibility panel, **click the FreeFlow Dev.app row, then click the minus button** in the bottom-left to remove the entry entirely
- Then in the FreeFlow setup wizard, advance or hit the re-check button. macOS will pop a fresh prompt → **Open System Settings** → this time it adds a new entry with the right signature and the toggle will actually take

Same trick will work for Screen Recording if it gets stuck — minus button, re-trigger from the app, fresh entry sticks.

This is also exactly why the "wipe and rebuild" cycle keeps biting us: ad-hoc signing means every rebuild produces a new signature hash, so TCC sees it as a different app each time. A real signing identity would make this go away. Worth setting up at some point.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 6:04 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 6:04 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

2026-04-24 6:04 PM  Claude: Two things you spotted, both real:

**1. Setup wizard exists.** I missed that — apologies. The fix is much smaller than I said: just auto-launch the existing setup wizard on cold start when permissions or API key are missing. That is a 5-line change in App.swift, not a UX rebuild. File as a separate PR after we are done with the spinner.

**2. Toggle does nothing.** Classic stale TCC entry. The row is left over from a prior build with a different signature, so flipping the toggle is operating on a ghost. The fix:

- In that Accessibility panel, **click the FreeFlow Dev.app row, then click the minus button** in the bottom-left to remove the entry entirely
- Then in the FreeFlow setup wizard, advance or hit the re-check button. macOS will pop a fresh prompt → **Open System Settings** → this time it adds a new entry with the right signature and the toggle will actually take

Same trick will work for Screen Recording if it gets stuck — minus button, re-trigger from the app, fresh entry sticks.

This is also exactly why the "wipe and rebuild" cycle keeps biting us: ad-hoc signing means every rebuild produces a new signature hash, so TCC sees it as a different app each time. A real signing identity would make this go away. Worth setting up at some point.

2026-04-24 6:04 PM  Claude: Yes. The "FreeFlow Dev: no identity found" error every time we build is because the Makefile defaults `CODESIGN_IDENTITY ?= FreeFlow Dev` — it expects a self-signed cert with that exact name in your keychain, and neither the MBP nor the Studio has one. So we have been falling back to ad-hoc, which gives every rebuild a different signature hash, which is why TCC keeps treating each build as a brand new app and the toggle does not stick.

**Two fix paths:**

1. **GUI, 30 seconds, you do it.** Keychain Access → Certificate Assistant → Create a Certificate. Name: "FreeFlow Dev". Identity Type: Self Signed Root. Certificate Type: Code Signing. Click through. Done. Future builds use it automatically.
2. **Scripted, I do it.** I can run openssl plus `security` to generate the cert and trust it for code signing on the MBP. Needs one `sudo` for the trust step (you will see the password prompt).

Either way, once that cert exists, the Makefile signs FreeFlow Dev with a stable identity, the signature hash stays the same across rebuilds, and TCC grants persist. The wipe-and-toggle dance goes away.

Want me to script it on the MBP, or do the GUI yourself?

2026-04-24 6:05 PM  Claude:
  *Bash:* Generate and trust FreeFlow Dev self-signed cert on MBP

2026-04-24 6:07 PM  Claude: That **is** the Apple Development cert — the one we keep seeing in `security find-identity` as `Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)`. Xcode auto-created it when you signed in with your Apple ID. It is exactly the right cert to use.

The reason it fails over SSH is the **private key's access control**: by default Xcode-created keys require user authorization (TouchID or password prompt) every time `codesign` wants to use them. SSH cannot trigger that prompt, which is what `errSecInternalComponent` means.

**One-time fix, has to be done from your MBP Terminal directly (not SSH):**

```
cd ~/apps/freeflow && make clean && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" make
```

Run that yourself in Terminal on the MBP. macOS will pop a keychain access dialog asking if `codesign` can access the private key. **Click "Always Allow"** (not "Allow Once"). After that, future SSH builds will just work because the access has been granted permanently.

Once that succeeds, I will replace /Applications and relaunch from here. And the cert hash stays stable across rebuilds, so TCC grants stick — no more wipe dance.

Skip the self-signed scripting path. The Apple Dev cert is better.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude: Two things you spotted, both real:

**1. Setup wizard exists.** I missed that — apologies. The fix is much smaller than I said: just auto-launch the existing setup wizard on cold start when permissions or API key are missing. That is a 5-line change in App.swift, not a UX rebuild. File as a separate PR after we are done with the spinner.

**2. Toggle does nothing.** Classic stale TCC entry. The row is left over from a prior build with a different signature, so flipping the toggle is operating on a ghost. The fix:

- In that Accessibility panel, **click the FreeFlow Dev.app row, then click the minus button** in the bottom-left to remove the entry entirely
- Then in the FreeFlow setup wizard, advance or hit the re-check button. macOS will pop a fresh prompt → **Open System Settings** → this time it adds a new entry with the right signature and the toggle will actually take

Same trick will work for Screen Recording if it gets stuck — minus button, re-trigger from the app, fresh entry sticks.

This is also exactly why the "wipe and rebuild" cycle keeps biting us: ad-hoc signing means every rebuild produces a new signature hash, so TCC sees it as a different app each time. A real signing identity would make this go away. Worth setting up at some point.

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude: Yes. The "FreeFlow Dev: no identity found" error every time we build is because the Makefile defaults `CODESIGN_IDENTITY ?= FreeFlow Dev` — it expects a self-signed cert with that exact name in your keychain, and neither the MBP nor the Studio has one. So we have been falling back to ad-hoc, which gives every rebuild a different signature hash, which is why TCC keeps treating each build as a brand new app and the toggle does not stick.

**Two fix paths:**

1. **GUI, 30 seconds, you do it.** Keychain Access → Certificate Assistant → Create a Certificate. Name: "FreeFlow Dev". Identity Type: Self Signed Root. Certificate Type: Code Signing. Click through. Done. Future builds use it automatically.
2. **Scripted, I do it.** I can run openssl plus `security` to generate the cert and trust it for code signing on the MBP. Needs one `sudo` for the trust step (you will see the password prompt).

Either way, once that cert exists, the Makefile signs FreeFlow Dev with a stable identity, the signature hash stays the same across rebuilds, and TCC grants persist. The wipe-and-toggle dance goes away.

Want me to script it on the MBP, or do the GUI yourself?

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude:

2026-04-24 6:11 PM  Claude: That **is** the Apple Development cert — the one we keep seeing in `security find-identity` as `Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)`. Xcode auto-created it when you signed in with your Apple ID. It is exactly the right cert to use.

The reason it fails over SSH is the **private key's access control**: by default Xcode-created keys require user authorization (TouchID or password prompt) every time `codesign` wants to use them. SSH cannot trigger that prompt, which is what `errSecInternalComponent` means.

**One-time fix, has to be done from your MBP Terminal directly (not SSH):**

```
cd ~/apps/freeflow && make clean && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" make
```

Run that yourself in Terminal on the MBP. macOS will pop a keychain access dialog asking if `codesign` can access the private key. **Click "Always Allow"** (not "Allow Once"). After that, future SSH builds will just work because the access has been granted permanently.

Once that succeeds, I will replace /Applications and relaunch from here. And the cert hash stays stable across rebuilds, so TCC grants stick — no more wipe dance.

Skip the self-signed scripting path. The Apple Dev cert is better.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: Classic macOS TCC gotcha. The toggle looks on, but because the app was just rebuilt with a different signature (ad-hoc instead of whatever it was signed with before), macOS is treating this as a new app that happens to share a bundle ID with a previously granted one. The permission record is tied to the code signature, not the name.

Fastest fix: **click the FreeFlow Dev.app toggle OFF, then back ON.** That re-registers it against the new signature. You should not have to restart the app.

If that does not clear it, the nuclear option is to reset the TCC record so the prompt comes back fresh and you grant it. Want me to run that?

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: [April 24, 2026 5:32 PM]
Session: freeflow: Investigate missing hammer icons in FreeFlow

Yes. At one second the processing phase is easy to miss on short phrases. To really see the state change you want to force the processing window to be long enough to watch the swap happen. A few ways to force that:

**Give it a long paragraph.** Dictate 30-60 seconds of speech — a multi-sentence story, a section of a book, a long message. Transcription scales with audio length, so a minute of speech typically means 3-5 seconds of processing. Long enough to see the waveform freeze, swap to spinner, and eventually insert.

**Or, easier to observe:** point the app at a slower transcription model or a longer-running post-processing model if you have one configured. That extends the processing window without you having to talk for a minute.

**Or, the cheat:** I can temporarily add a `sleep` into the transcription path just for testing, then rip it out before the PR. Hardcoded 5-second delay in the transcribing state so you can clearly see the transition every time. Want me to do that for a test build?

My recommendation: try the long paragraph first, the real behavior is what matters. If that still does not read well, I will add the test delay.

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: I see the right build (v0.3.0-spinner-2) and you are absolutely right — dumping the user into a menu bar full of orange/red warning items plus a floating system prompt is rough first-run UX. There is already a `SetupView.swift` in the codebase (you can see "Re-run Setup..." in the menu), but it is not auto-triggered on a fresh install. Mic and screen recording may prompt next. Once all green, dictate that long paragraph and watch the swap.

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: Two things you spotted, both real:

**1. Setup wizard exists.** I missed that — apologies. The fix is much smaller than I said: just auto-launch the existing setup wizard on cold start when permissions or API key are missing. That is a 5-line change in App.swift, not a UX rebuild. File as a separate PR after we are done with the spinner.

**2. Toggle does nothing.** Classic stale TCC entry. The row is left over from a prior build with a different signature, so flipping the toggle is operating on a ghost. The fix:

- In that Accessibility panel, **click the FreeFlow Dev.app row, then click the minus button** in the bottom-left to remove the entry entirely
- Then in the FreeFlow setup wizard, advance or hit the re-check button. macOS will pop a fresh prompt → **Open System Settings** → this time it adds a new entry with the right signature and the toggle will actually take

Same trick will work for Screen Recording if it gets stuck — minus button, re-trigger from the app, fresh entry sticks.

This is also exactly why the "wipe and rebuild" cycle keeps biting us: ad-hoc signing means every rebuild produces a new signature hash, so TCC sees it as a different app each time. A real signing identity would make this go away. Worth setting up at some point.

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: Yes. The "FreeFlow Dev: no identity found" error every time we build is because the Makefile defaults `CODESIGN_IDENTITY ?= FreeFlow Dev` — it expects a self-signed cert with that exact name in your keychain, and neither the MBP nor the Studio has one. So we have been falling back to ad-hoc, which gives every rebuild a different signature hash, which is why TCC keeps treating each build as a brand new app and the toggle does not stick.

**Two fix paths:**

1. **GUI, 30 seconds, you do it.** Keychain Access → Certificate Assistant → Create a Certificate. Name: "FreeFlow Dev". Identity Type: Self Signed Root. Certificate Type: Code Signing. Click through. Done. Future builds use it automatically.
2. **Scripted, I do it.** I can run openssl plus `security` to generate the cert and trust it for code signing on the MBP. Needs one `sudo` for the trust step (you will see the password prompt).

Either way, once that cert exists, the Makefile signs FreeFlow Dev with a stable identity, the signature hash stays the same across rebuilds, and TCC grants persist. The wipe-and-toggle dance goes away.

Want me to script it on the MBP, or do the GUI yourself?

2026-04-24 6:17 PM  Claude: That **is** the Apple Development cert — the one we keep seeing in `security find-identity` as `Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)`. Xcode auto-created it when you signed in with your Apple ID. It is exactly the right cert to use.

The reason it fails over SSH is the **private key's access control**: by default Xcode-created keys require user authorization (TouchID or password prompt) every time `codesign` wants to use them. SSH cannot trigger that prompt, which is what `errSecInternalComponent` means.

**One-time fix, has to be done from your MBP Terminal directly (not SSH):**

```
cd ~/apps/freeflow && make clean && CODESIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Development: ojhurst@gmail.com (FKLXWDKTW3)" make
```

Run that yourself in Terminal on the MBP. macOS will pop a keychain access dialog asking if `codesign` can access the private key. **Click "Always Allow"** (not "Allow Once"). After that, future SSH builds will just work because the access has been granted permanently.

Once that succeeds, I will replace /Applications and relaunch from here. And the cert hash stays stable across rebuilds, so TCC grants stick — no more wipe dance.

Skip the self-signed scripting path. The Apple Dev cert is better.

Session: freeflow: Add FreeFlow spinner animation during audio

Shift noted, still on the same FreeFlow spinner thread, just retitled. That makes a lot of sense and it is close to what the code already does.

Here is how I would reconcile your thinking with what is there:

The current flow already has two "indicator delay" knobs. When you release, the waveform freezes at last-seen level for a brief moment (so instant transcriptions never flash anything — the insert just happens). Only if transcription takes longer than `transcribingIndicatorDelay` does the processing indicator appear. So the spinner would only ever show on the slow ones. That matches your "if it is going to be more than a second, then do the spinner" exactly.

**My proposal:**
- Find the current `transcribingIndicatorDelay` value. If it is already around one second, leave it alone. If it is shorter, bump it to about one second so fast transcriptions stay clean.
- Replace `ProcessingWaveformView` with a native circular `ProgressView()`, small, white, sized for the pill.
- Everything else stays, including the "off" audio cue when you release.

Net effect: short ones, you hear the pop and the text inserts, no spinner. Long ones, you see a real spinner that reads clearly as "waiting, not listening."

Want me to go build that? I will check the delay value first so I can tell you what it is set to now before changing it.
